verses.commentary: Pharisees and scribes — from Jerusalem this time. Jerusalem's religious authorities investigate the Galilean rabbi. The conflict escalates institutionally.
'Tradition of the elders' (paradosin ton presbyteron, παράδοσιν τῶν πρεσβυτέρων) — oral law, hand-washing rituals beyond Torah. Disciples don't comply. The accusation centers on tradition, not Torah.
Jesus counter-questions: 'Why do you break the command of God for your tradition?' The accusers themselves stand accused. The tradition violates the Torah it claims to interpret.
Citation of Exodus 20:12 and 21:17 — honor your father and mother. Capital crime to curse parents under Mosaic Law.
Their workaround: 'Corban' — declare resources 'devoted to God,' thereby exempting them from supporting parents. Loophole that wears piety as a mask.
Jesus' verdict: 'You nullify (akyroo, ἀκυρόω, void) the word of God for the sake of your tradition.' Tradition has overridden Torah. Religious bookkeeping at the expense of filial care.
'Hypocrites.' Isaiah 29:13 quoted — Isaiah saw your kind.
'These people honor me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me.' Lip-piety divorced from heart-disposition. The diagnostic recurs throughout Matthew.
Worship in vain — teachings as commandments of men. The Pharisees' authority is human, not divine. The accusation lands hard.
Jesus turns to crowd: 'Hear and understand.' Public teaching follows private rebuke.
Not what enters the mouth defiles, but what comes out. Reversal of purity logic — defilement is internal, not external.
Disciples report: 'You offended (eskandalisthesan, ἐσκανδαλίσθησαν) the Pharisees.' Jesus' teaching causes scandal even where it should cause clarity.
'Every plant not planted by my Father will be pulled up.' Pharisaic doctrine is not from God; its uprooting is certain. Botanical image of judgment.
'Let them alone (aphete, ἄφετε). Blind guides of the blind — both fall in a pit.' Withdrawal from impossible dialogue. Pharisees beyond persuasion at this stage.
Peter asks for explanation of the parable about defilement.
'Even you still without understanding?' Jesus expresses surprise at disciples' slowness. The interpretive lag is real.
Food enters stomach, exits the body. Digestive system processes food without spiritual residue. The body is neither sanctified nor defiled by food.
What comes out of the mouth comes from the heart. Speech as heart-overflow (compare 12:34). The diagnostic recurs.
Out of the heart: evil thoughts, murder, adultery, sexual immorality, theft, false witness, blasphemy. Catalog of sins matches Decalogue order (5-9). The mouth is the heart's exhaust pipe.
These defile. Eating with unwashed hands does not. Moral interior outranks ritual exterior. The category-correction radical.
Withdraw (anachoreo) to Tyre and Sidon — Gentile territory. Jesus retreats north into pagan land.

'Canaanite' woman — Matthew uses the archaic ethnic name (Mark says Syrophoenician). Calls him 'Lord, Son of David' — Gentile addressing Israel's Messiah with Israel's title. Her daughter demon-possessed.
Jesus answers nothing. Disciples want her sent away. The silence is unusual — and instructive.
'I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel.' Same restriction as 10:6. The Messiah's mission is initially defined ethnically.
She kneels: 'Lord, help me.' Persistence reduced to three words. Faith does not negotiate.

'Not right to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs.' Tough metaphor: Israel's children, Gentile dogs. Yet 'dogs' (kynaria, κυναρία — diminutive, household puppies) — mitigates. The metaphor opens a door it seems to close.
'Yes, Lord, even the dogs eat the crumbs from their masters' table.' She accepts the metaphor and extends it. Subordinate position requested, not equality demanded. Crumbs sufficient.

'O woman, great is your faith!' (megale sou he pistis, μεγάλη σου ἡ πίστις). Twice in Matthew Jesus marvels at faith — both Gentiles (centurion 8:10, this woman). Daughter healed instantly. The Messiah's gift crosses the boundary on the woman's persistent faith.
Back to Galilee, up the mountain, sits. Another mountain teaching scene gathers.
Great crowds bring lame, blind, mute, crippled, and many others. Mass healing event. Wholesale restoration.
Crowd amazed, 'glorified the God of Israel' — phrase suggesting Gentile observers (compare 4:25). The God of Israel praised by outsiders.
Three days with him, nothing to eat. Compassion (splagchnizomai, σπλαγχνίζομαι) — gut mercy again. Concern for collapse on the journey home.
Disciples again: 'Where can we get bread?' Same question as before the feeding of 5000. Memory does not transfer.
Seven loaves, a few small fish. Seven (perfection) and a few. The numbers differ from chapter 14, suggesting a distinct event.
Crowd sits on the ground. No grass mentioned — Gentile region in dry season, perhaps.
Same fourfold action: took, gave thanks, broke, gave to disciples. Eucharistic pattern repeated.
All satisfied. Seven baskets (spyridas, σπυρίδας — different word, larger basket) of leftovers. Possibly symbolically 'seven' for Gentile nations.
Four thousand men, besides women and children. Second feeding miracle. The numerology may signal Gentile inclusion (seven loaves, seven baskets versus twelve before).
Crowd dismissed, boat to Magadan (region near Capernaum). Jesus turns back south.
